title: IT-to-OT Perimeter Firewall Rule Violation
spl:
index=ot sourcetype="ics:firewall"
| where (src_zone="IT" AND dst_zone="OT")
| lookup ot_perimeter_allow_rules rule_id OUTPUT allowed_description
| where isnull(allowed_description) AND action="permit"
| stats count earliest(_time) as first_seen values(dest_port) as ports by src, dest, rule_id
| sort - count
description: Detects traffic crossing the IT/OT boundary that violates approved rules, preserving segmentation and reducing ransomware lateral movement into control systems.
value: Detects traffic crossing the IT/OT boundary that violates approved rules, preserving segmentation and reducing ransomware lateral movement into control systems.
implementation: Consume normalized perimeter logs in Splunk ES. Reconcile rule_id with change-managed policies. Use for continuous compliance evidence.
visualization: Sankey IT‚ÜíOT flows, Table of violating rules, Trend of unique new flows.
knownFalsePositives: OT firewall denies from authorized engineering access, vendor remote support, or scheduled maintenance VPN sessions can look like policy violations in raw logs when the slot was actually approved ‚ÄĒ compare to the change ticket time box.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚ÄĘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151).
‚ÄĘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype="ics:firewall"` with `rule_id`, `action`, `src_zone`, `dst_zone`.
‚ÄĘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Configure data collection
Consume normalized perimeter logs in Splunk ES. Reconcile rule_id with change-managed policies. Use for continuous compliance evidence.

Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="ics:firewall"
| where (src_zone="IT" AND dst_zone="OT")
| lookup ot_perimeter_allow_rules rule_id OUTPUT allowed_description
| where isnull(allowed_description) AND action="permit"
| stats count earliest(_time) as first_seen values(dest_port) as ports by src, dest, rule_id
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**IT-to-OT Perimeter Firewall Rule Violation** ‚ÄĒ Detects traffic crossing the IT/OT boundary that violates approved rules, preserving segmentation and reducing ransomware lateral movement into control systems.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="ics:firewall"` with `rule_id`, `action`, `src_zone`, `dst_zone`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚ÄĒrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: ics:firewall. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚ÄĘ Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="ics:firewall". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚ÄĘ Filters the current rows with `where (src_zone="IT" AND dst_zone="OT")` ‚ÄĒ typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚ÄĘ Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
‚ÄĘ Filters the current rows with `where isnull(allowed_description) AND action="permit"` ‚ÄĒ typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚ÄĘ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src, dest, rule_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚ÄĘ Orders rows with `sort` ‚ÄĒ combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Validate
Compare failed or disabled searches in Splunk to the **OT Security Add-on** configuration UI and the **Saved Searches** list so names and schedules line up. Spot-check one failure against `scheduler.log` around the run time.



Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Sankey IT‚ÜíOT flows, Table of violating rules, Trend of unique new flows.
grandmaExplanation: We enforce the rule that IT-style traffic is not allowed straight into the plant without the right hop and approval.
